title: Bridge Paper: Exploring Habits in Anorexia Nervosa: Promise, Pitfalls, and Progress.
author: Frontier Science / Hermes T2

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Mechanicality** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge thesis:** The mechanical mind — neuroscience of automaticity revealing how we become trapped in behavioral loops.
**Phase context:** Habit, automaticity, default mode network, compulsive behavior

Paper: Exploring Habits in Anorexia Nervosa: Promise, Pitfalls, and Progress.
DOI: 10.1007/s11920-025-01588-7
Abstract: PURPOSE OF REVIEW: Habits, characterized by automaticity and insensitivity to outcomes, may be key to the persistence of maladaptive behaviors in anorexia nervosa (AN). This review examines the status of habit research in AN, focusing on insights from task-based assessments. Habits, characterized by automaticity and insensitivity to outcomes, may be key to the persistence of maladaptive behaviors in anorexia nervosa (AN). This review examines the status of habit research in AN, focusing on insights from task-based assessments. RECENT FINDINGS: Findings indicate dysfunction in the frontostriatal circuits associated with habitual and goal-directed behaviors, with some studies linking neural disturbances to habit measures or clinically relevant behaviors. Heightened habitual tendencies in AN have consistently been reported using self-reports, while research utilizing experimental paradigms has yielded mixed results and efforts to capture real-world habits in AN remain limited. Some experimental paradigms appear more sensitive than others, but all face challenges associated with studying habits in the lab. Promising new approaches will need to be adopted and efforts made to capture real-world habits. Understanding which habits are problematic, when in illness and for whom they dominate, could make good on the promise of habit-focused treatments for AN.
